---
title: Automated Assessment of Residual Plots with Computer Vision Models
subtitle: Response to reviewers for JCGS-25-535.R1
date: today
filters:
   - latex-environment
environments: [reviewer]
commands: [deemph, review]
format: 
  pdf:
    include-in-header:
      text: | 
       \usepackage[dvipsnames]{xcolor}
       \newcommand{\review}[1]{{\color{violet} #1}}
       \newenvironment{reviewer}{
       \color{violet}}
       {}
       \renewenvironment{quote}{%
        \list{}{%
          \leftmargin0.5cm   % this is the adjusting screw
          \itshape
          \rightmargin\leftmargin
        }
        \item\relax
       }
       {\endlist}

---

We thank the editor and the reviewer for their constructive comments that have improved this paper. We have addressed all comments, colored in [purple]{.review}. In addition, we also include an annotated version of the paper (`diff.pdf`) with the difference between the original and revised versions produced using `latexdiff`. 

## Reviewer


**1 Overall Comments**

::: reviewer

The idea of the paper is interesting and potentially useful. However, I think that the paper needs to
be significantly revised to be solid. The paper contains two major ideas for residual diagnosis. One
is the use of KL distance based measure $D$ for discriminate good models and wrong models. The
other idea is to use computer vision models to estimate $D$ with $\hat{D}$. The methodology part of the
paper needs to be separated clearly for these two tasks. The paper directly reports the performance
of $\hat{D}$ but didnot verify the goodness of $D$ itself. _Although we see reasonable performance of $\hat{D}$, the
goodness of $D$ is still doubtful to me._ The measure $D$ is a logarithm of a sum of KL distances of $n$
observations. Therefore, the raw value of $D$ is affected by the sample size $n$, the degree and types
of model violation, and many other factors such as the number of predictors. Hence, the raw value
of $D$ may not be informative to detect model violation unless their distributions are calibrated by
some methods. This may be why the authors use bootstrap to generate null and boot distributions
of $D$ to come up with a valid p-value. Unfortunately the description of the bootstrap procedure
is unclear. It is suggested that the authors report the performance of the test p-value based on
original $D$ along with computer-vision-based $\hat{D}$, and describe the bootstrap procedure more clearly.

:::

[TODO]


**2 Technical Critiques and Queries**

::: reviewer

pg 8, line 29:

The authors use the word “certain”. This makes it sound as though it is unknown. It is wellknown that in OLS, $Q$ follows a rank-deficient multivariate normal, and $\|\hat{e}\|^2$ follows $\chi^2$ with df = $n - p$.

:::

[TODO]

::: reviewer

pg 10, eq 3 and eq 2:

Can the authors explain why the specific form $\log(1 + D_{KL})$ is used? I don’t see that eq 3
explains this form. Why +1 and why log?

:::

[TODO]

::: reviewer

About the $D$:

The features of $D$ are unclear to me. Does its distribution depend on sample size and model
violation types (for example, non-normality, omission of predictors, dependency)? Do the
proposed tests try to get rid of this complicated distribution by using bootstrapping? I suspect
that the $D$ or $\hat{D}$ strongly depends on sample 

:::

[TODO]


::: reviewer

pg 13: line 57: "we can apply bootstrapping to the estimated distance $D$":

The bootstrapping procedure is unclear. How is the bootstrapping for residuals performed? To
detect model deviation, we need to have $\hat{D}$ from the null distribution. Do the authors generate
datasets from the fitted model for a given dataset? I raised this question for the authors’ R0
version. The response letter said, "Specifically, assuming the fitted regression model is correct,
we generate null residuals using residual rotation". _How can we assume the fitted model is
correct? How can a rotation of residuals from a wrong model produce residuals from fitting a true
distribution?_ In addition, the phrases "true residual plot" and "null residual plot" are used often
by the authors. These phrases are technically confusing. A more detailed explaination is needed.

:::

[TODO]


::: reviewer

pg 14: line 42: "The model begins with an input layer of shape n × h × w × 3, capable
of handling n RGB images".

What do n, h, w, and 3 mean? Why do the authors use RGB images for computer vision? Is
there color in a residual plot?

:::

[TODO]


::: reviewer

pg 16, Sections 4, 6, 7:

These sections are all about how to generate training datasets with known $D$ to train the
computer vision model $f_{CV}$. I think these three sections should be merged together into a
single one. Section 5 should be moved after or before this section to explain how to use $D$ or
$\hat{D}$ for obtain a test p-value.

:::

[TODO]


::: reviewer

Sec 8.2.1:

The comparison with human visual inference is hard for me to follow. Who are the humans
who made the visual decision? Are the participants experienced in looking at residual plots?
Also, what’s the difference between a “true residual plot” and a “null residual plot”? It seems
to me that human judgment is subjective. It would be OK not to compare with human visual
decision at all.

:::

[TODO]


::: reviewer

Fig 8: “Finally, the power curves in Figure 8 are fitted using logistic regression without
intercepts and with an offset of log(0.05/0.95) to ensure a 5% rejection rate under H0.”

This way to report rejection rate is very wierd to me. Why don’t the authors simply report the
rejection rate, ie, proportion of rejections with $p < 0.05$, but fitting a model to the decisions. In
addition, it seems that this is based on true $D$ not $\hat{D}$. I think it is necessary to report the direct
rejection rates of $\hat{D}$ and $D$. This may

:::

[TODO]
